name: Mutative Assembly Inversion (MAI)
description: Find the unique register inputs R0 and R1 in [0, 255] that cause a self-modifying assembly program to terminate with a specified target register state within a maximum step limit.
capability_claim: Evaluates logic-tracking, execution-emulation, symbolic analysis, and program-inversion capabilities of frontier models on completely novel, self-modifying, non-standard computer architectures.

closest_existing_benchmarks.name: FrontierMath / AIME
closest_existing_benchmarks.reason: Evaluates mathematical logic, but does not test programmatic execution state tracking under dynamic self-modification.

closest_existing_benchmarks.name: SWE-bench / LiveCodeBench
closest_existing_benchmarks.reason: Evaluates standard coding and debugging, but does not test low-level custom assembly reverse engineering or self-modifying machine emulation.

closest_existing_benchmarks.name: OSWorld / GAIA
closest_existing_benchmarks.reason: Evaluates computer use and agent tool reasoning, whereas MAI is a pure logical reasoning and constraint satisfaction task.

grading_method: Exact match on unique input pair (R0=X,R1=Y)
input_format: JSONL containing initial SMRM instructions (as integers and assembly text), initial constant registers, target final register values, and step limit.
output_format: Exactly R0=X,R1=Y where X and Y are decimal integer values in [0, 255].
